straight_beat.name: Chet Pazulski
straight_beat.column:
The Pirates lost yesterday, 5-4 to the Nationals, but let’s not let that cloud the fact that they’re still in this thing. 10-7, -1 in the NL Central. Yinzer math, boss. You don’t need a calculator to see that the team’s still got legs.  

The real story isn’t the loss—it’s the way they fought back. Nationals took the lead in the seventh, but the Pirates didn’t fold

optimist.name: Franny Harsanyi
optimist.column:
The Pirates lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on that. Let’s talk about Paul Skenes. That kid’s got the fire of a thousand stevedores, and I’m not just saying that because he’s a pitcher. I’m saying it because he’s got the heart of Steve Blass and the hustle of Smoky Burgess, all wrapped in a modern-day package. Skenes threw 102 pitches, and I almost called my daughter to come over and watch. That’s the feeling that comes back. That’s why we keep showing up.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: 102 pitches? That’s a lot. But here’s the thing—when you’ve got a pitcher who can make every pitch matter, every pitch count, that’s not a problem. It’s a testament. Skenes didn’t just throw 102; he made us believe again. He’s got that rare mix of grit and grace, the kind of stuff that makes you think, “This is why we came back to the ballpark.”  

And let’s not forget the rest of the team. The Pirates are 10-7, and while that’s not where we want to be, it’s not where we’re headed either. The front office has been busy, and I’ll bet you a nickel they’ve got a plan. You can’t build a contender overnight, but you can start with a kid like Skenes and a bunch of guys who’re willing to fight for every inch.

pessimist.name: Wally Horvath
pessimist.column:
The Pirates lost again. Another 5-4 defeat, another reminder that this team is a glorified tax write-off with a roster attached. Washington’s bats did the damage, sure, but let’s not kid ourselves—this isn’t about the Nationals. It’s about the Pirates, and their inability to do the basics.  

The bullpen, that cursed collection of hopefuls and has-beens, let the game slip away. You know the script: a lead in the late innings, a shaky pitch, a walk-off home run, and suddenly the Nationals are celebrating like they’ve won the World Series. The Pirates’ relievers have turned into a circus act, with each outing feeling like a new episode of “How to Lose a Game in 30 Seconds.” Skenes pitched the game of his life, but what’s the point? Nutting’s
